encodingDesc.ab: Converted from a Word document for Pylon.

div.head: 1. P.Hamb. graec. 150: List of distributions
div.p: Descr.: A small square papyrus sheet of a light brown color, written along the fibers. All four margins are preserved; the right edge is slightly worn. The text consists of seven lines written by a single, non-cursive, elementary hand. The verso is blank.

The papyrus was part of a lot purchased by the Staats- und Universitätsbibliothek Hamburg in 1907 from Mohamed Abdallah, a lot probably deriving from Oxyrhynchus (for the acquisition details, see Essler 2021: 195–197, 200). The document is a list of distributions recorded in διπλᾶ, ‘doubles,’ a measure of liquid or solid commodities. In the present context, these entries most probably refer to wine. The text ends with the formula ὁμοῦ εἰς δαπάνας (‘altogether for expenses’) and a total sum. The numbers are preceded twice by personal names, twice by professions, once by a term that may have legal significance, although its precise meaning remains uncertain. The random list of recipients implies that the account was an internal record, perhaps of an estate.

The διπλοῦν served as a unit of account for various items from the 2nd to 7th centuries (Worp 2000: 146–147), including soap, must, pickled goods, meat, and even coins,
See P.Oxy. 16 2051.21 (6th–7th c.); P.Erl. 111.11 (5th–6th c.); P.Oxy. 3 520.6 (2nd c.); P.Erl. 86.7 (4th c.; TM 33367); P.Oxy. 34 2729.11 (4th c.) respectively, Worp 2000: 147. but predominantly wine.
This term is also encountered in a substantial number of Coptic documentary texts, almost always as a measure of wine, see Worp 2000: 148. It was not a fixed measure and fluctuated over time and across regions, varying between two and four litres in Byzantine Egypt (Worp 2000: 147). Terms such as διπλοῦν could denote vessels of relevant capacity (see Kruit and Worp 1999).
See Kruit and Worp 1999: 113, 116. By the Byzantine period, the sextarius emerged as the standard unit for liquids in Egypt, whereby container names (including διπλοῦν) were expressed as its multiples (see Kruit and Worp 1999: 119–120).

The itemized figures do not correspond to the total of expenses in the amount of 14 dipla.
I would like to thank Dr. Michael W. Zellmann-Rohrer for careful reading and advice that helped the interpretation of this account. Several reasons are possible. Since the document is complete and undamaged, this discrepancy cannot be explained by loss of text. It is possible, however, that some items included in the final total were recorded in a separate document. A further hypothesis would be a scribal miscalculation, although this is difficult to reconcile with the figures preserved in ll. 1–5, which total either 8 or 11 dipla depending on whether the partially preserved letter in l. 2 is read as α or, more probably, as δ. Even so, the discrepancy with the total of expenses would still amount to 6 or 3 διπλᾶ, which seems substantial in such a brief list. Another possibility is that the total included a separate expense entry unrelated to the preceding items. The logic is difficult to follow, but for the author it must have made sense.

note.p: 2. ταπητᾶς: a carpet-maker (or merchant), also ταπητάριος (see Pruneti 2012: 149–155). The nominative is noteworthy, since the context would suggest a dative ταπητᾷ (see e.g. P.Oxy. 42 3044.5 and P.Iand. 8 153.14). This may indicate an inconsistency in the grammatical shaping of the entries.

4. ἐνέχυρ<όγρ>αφα: the only reading compatible with the visible traces, even though it is problematic in the present context, where the entries seem to refer to recipients and thus call for an animate designation.

5. Ἀφυγγι: a variant of the name Ἀπύγχις (TM NamVar 589), a form apparently peculiar to Oxyrhynchus in the 4th–5th centuries (P.Oxy. 16 1870; 17 2156.19; 82 5330.4; P.Lond. 5 1777.4; see also Gonis 2004: 197).

div.head: 2. P.Vindob. G 28987: List of payments
div.p: Descr.: This is a light brown fragment consisting of five partially preserved lines written on the recto. The verso is blank. Only the bottom margin of the papyrus is preserved. The script is cursive and slightly sloping.

The document contains a list of payments in solidi (νομίσματα), including three entries for physicians. These entries mention a chief physician and two regular physicians. The number of solidi is preserved only in l. 5.

The title ἀρχίατρος is attested from the Hellenistic period onward and continued in use under the Roman and Byzantine empires for physicians of particularly high rank,
An example is Caius Stertinius Xenophon, a prominent ἀρχίατρος to the emperor and his family (Samama 2003: 42–43, 257–259). Other cases from the late Roman period include Stephanos, ἐνδοξοτάτος ἀρχιατρός, see Samama 2003: 67, 470. whether at court or in civic service
See the inscription from the reign of Antiochus III (Bull. Epigr. 1971, no. 600, p. 427; see Nutton 1977: 193–194) and two Delian inscriptions (I.Delos 1547; 1573, see Samama 2003: 208–210) mentioning Crateros (court of Antiochus IX) and Papias of Amisus (court of Mithridates VI Eupator) respectively. (Marasco 1996: 445–447, 459–460; Samama 2003: 62–68).
A case worth mentioning here is CIG 4678, which mentions ἀρχιητρός Nicanor, a steward of Ptolemy II Philadelphus (for the discussion, see Hirt Raj 2006: 57–60). It could also function as an honorary designation.
An inscription from Alexandria dated to 7 CE attests archiatros as what has been taken to be an honorary title conferred by ‘an association of physicians’ (see Zimonyi 2018: 231–232). Similar honorary uses are attested in inscriptions from the Greek East between the 2nd and 4th centuries, including for doctors attached to private institutions such as athletic associations (TAM V.2 1097; Zimonyi 2018: fn. 29; Aytaçlar and Özenir 2024: fn. 40). The title could also reflect family prestige, as perhaps in the case of Augusta from Lycaonia, called ἀρχιατρίνη, probably through her husband’s title (MAMA VII 566; see Samama 2003: 442–443). Epigraphic evidence also reveals families of municipal archiatri, see Nutton 1977: 200–202; Samama 2003: 19–20 and Zimonyi 2018: fn. 33. In the document discussed here, the title most probably serves to mark an internal hierarchy within a single institutional setting, distinguishing a ‘chief physician’ from his subordinate colleagues. In Roman and Byzantine Egypt the title archiatros does not itself denote a public physician and may instead imply a role of supervision or seniority (Raschel 2024: 127, 132); by contrast, the title demosios iatros in Roman Egypt is attested chiefly in medico-legal contexts (Gad 2012: 265–273).

The salaries of the physicians must have been relatively high.
See Underwood 2018: 342–384; Samama 2003: 43–44; Hirt Raj 2006: 99–101. One example is CPR 19 18.9 (5th c.), a list of expenses mentioning a horse-physician whose payment amounts to two carats. Other examples are P.Ant. 3 202.15 (6th–7th c.) and P.Cair.Masp. 1 67057.13 (6th c.). The latter is a record of expenses of the city, which included the salaries of the town officials, inter alia, 25 solidi per year for the physician. It is about two solidi per month, an amount similar to that earned by estate managers (Underwood 2018: 360). In PSI 8 891.19 (5th–6th c.), a physician Ioannes received a payment of one solidus. In SB 26 16446.3 (4th c.), a donkey physician (ὀνοϊατρός) is paid three argyria in an unspecified coin.

The ἀρχίατρος had to be paid more than a regular doctor. In P.Cair.Masp. 2 67151 (6th c.), the annual salary of the chief physician of Antinoopolis, Flavius Phoibammon, son of Euprepeios, amounted to 60 solidi (ll. 289–290). This sum is less generous than those known from Justinian's constitution, according to which a chief physician is expected to earn 99 solidi per year, his assistant 70 solidi per year, and the remaining three physicians 150 solidi per year (50 solidi each, see Underwood 2018: 360).

Given this context, the payment of two nomismata to both the ἀρχίατρος and the ἰατρός in the papyrus below is unlikely to represent a full annual salary comparable to the 60 solidi of the archiatros of Antinoopolis (P.Cair.Masp. II 67151). Rather, it is better seen as an occasional fee or short-term installment.

note.p: 3. ]ι̣ ̣: the vertical line at the beginning of this verse is the final iota of a third-declension name in the dative, consistent with the dative case of ἀρχίατρος, and might have belonged to the doctor’s name.

listBibl.bibl: Aytaçlar, P.Ö., Özenir, S.A. (2024) ‘Gladiatorial Monuments from Thyateira,’ Gephyra 27: 33–42.
Essler, H. (2021) ‘Zur Entstehung der Hamburger Papyrussammlung,’ APF 67, 1: 166–208.
Gad, El-Sayed (2012) ‘The demosios iatros in Roman Egypt: a municipal position or a liturgic office?’ Actes du 26e Congrès international de papyrologie: 265–273.
Gonis, N. (2004) ‘Notes on Oxyrhynchus Papyri III,’ ZPE 150: 197–202.

Hirt Raj, M. (2006) ‘Médecins et malades de l’Égypte romaine. Étude socio-légale de la profession médicale et de ses praticiens du Ier au IVe siècle ap. J.-C.,’ Studies in Ancient Medicine 32. Leiden.
Kruit, N., Worp, K.A. (1999) ‘Metrological notes on measures and containers of liquids in Graeco-Roman and Byzantine Egypt,’ Archiv für Papyrusforschung und verwandte Gebiete 45: 96–127.
Marasco, G. (1996) ‘Les médecins de cour à l'époque hellénistique,’ Revue des Études Grecques 109, 2: 435–466.
Nutton, V. (1977) ‘Archiatri and the Medical Profession in Antiquity,’ Papers of the British School at Rome 45: 191–226.
Pruneti, P. (2012) ‘Lista nominativa dei fabbricanti di tappeti: le testimonianze dei papiri,’ AnalPap, 21–22, 149–155.
Raschel, C-L. (2024) ‘Les archiatroi, les médecins publics et l’hôpital en Égypte byzantine,’ Dialogues d’histoire ancienne, 50/2, 125–152.
Samama, E. (2003) Les médecins dans le monde grec. Sources épigraphiques sur la naissance d'un corps médical. Genève.
Underwood, N. (2018) ‘Medicine, Money, and Christian Rhetoric: The Socio-Economic Dimensions of Healthcare in Late Antiquity,’ Studies in Late Antiquity 2 (3): 342–384.
Worp, K.A. (2000) ‘A survey of ἁπλᾶ, δι(δι)πλᾶ and τριπλᾶ measures in the papyri,’ ZPE 131: 145–149.

Zimonyi, Á. (2018) ‘Archiatres id est medicus sapientissimus – Changes in the meaning of the term archiatros in the Roman Empire,’ in Sapiens Unicus Civis, Proceedings of International Conference on Classical Studies: 230–241.
Aytaçlar, P.Ö., Özenir, S.A. (2024) ‘Gladiatorial Monuments from Thyateira,’ Gephyra 27: 33–42.
Essler, H. (2021) ‘Zur Entstehung der Hamburger Papyrussammlung,’ APF 67, 1: 166–208.
Gad, El-Sayed (2012) ‘The demosios iatros in Roman Egypt: a municipal position or a liturgic office?’ Actes du 26e Congrès international de papyrologie: 265–273.
Gonis, N. (2004) ‘Notes on Oxyrhynchus Papyri III,’ ZPE 150: 197–202.

Hirt Raj, M. (2006) ‘Médecins et malades de l’Égypte romaine. Étude socio-légale de la profession médicale et de ses praticiens du Ier au IVe siècle ap. J.-C.,’ Studies in Ancient Medicine 32. Leiden.
Kruit, N., Worp, K.A. (1999) ‘Metrological notes on measures and containers of liquids in Graeco-Roman and Byzantine Egypt,’ Archiv für Papyrusforschung und verwandte Gebiete 45: 96–127.
Marasco, G. (1996) ‘Les médecins de cour à l'époque hellénistique,’ Revue des Études Grecques 109, 2: 435–466.
Nutton, V. (1977) ‘Archiatri and the Medical Profession in Antiquity,’ Papers of the British School at Rome 45: 191–226.
Pruneti, P. (2012) ‘Lista nominativa dei fabbricanti di tappeti: le testimonianze dei papiri,’ AnalPap, 21–22, 149–155.
Raschel, C-L. (2024) ‘Les archiatroi, les médecins publics et l’hôpital en Égypte byzantine,’ Dialogues d’histoire ancienne, 50/2, 125–152.
Samama, E. (2003) Les médecins dans le monde grec. Sources épigraphiques sur la naissance d'un corps médical. Genève.
Underwood, N. (2018) ‘Medicine, Money, and Christian Rhetoric: The Socio-Economic Dimensions of Healthcare in Late Antiquity,’ Studies in Late Antiquity 2 (3): 342–384.
Worp, K.A. (2000) ‘A survey of ἁπλᾶ, δι(δι)πλᾶ and τριπλᾶ measures in the papyri,’ ZPE 131: 145–149.

Zimonyi, Á. (2018) ‘Archiatres id est medicus sapientissimus – Changes in the meaning of the term archiatros in the Roman Empire,’ in Sapiens Unicus Civis, Proceedings of International Conference on Classical Studies: 230–241.

ταπητᾷταπητᾶς διπλᾶ δ
τῷ διάκονιδιακόνῳδιακον διπλᾶ β
ἐνέχυρόγραφα διπλᾶ β

διπλᾶ ιδ

div.p: To Anouthis, 2 double-jars To the carpet-maker, 4 double-jars To the deacon, 2 double-jars Pledge documents, 2 double-jars To Aphyngis, one double-jar Altogether for expenses, 14 double-jars

div.p: … of the record, solidi… to the chief physician X solidi… to the physician two solidi… to the physician two solidi…
